# [★★☆] Short Crypto

## SecretFunction^2

<details open>
<summary>
Description
</summary>

Legend has it that Gregory the Great—retired barista turned mad quaternion tinkerer—built a four-dimensional safe
guarded by hyper-caffeinated squirrels and locked his secret croissant recipe inside a swirling mashup of a, b, c, and d
raised to the 65537th power; now it’s up to you to brave his bamboozling “SecretFunction” arithmetic (u, v, and w
included at no extra charge) and decrypt his espresso-fueled masterpiece.

[`main.py`](main.py) [`message.txt`](message.txt)

</details>
<details>
<summary>
Solution
</summary>

Found a very similar CTF challenge
[write-up from SECCON CTF 2023](https://qiita.com/hiikunZ/items/e2ab86cf2792dd1394df#rsa-40-crypto). Using math from
that, it was possible to acquire the original $p$ and $q$.

After that, ChatGPT did crazy stuff to get $m$ out of the quarternion, see [`solve.py`](Solution/solve.py).

```
% time ./solve.py 
Step 1: Recovering p and q from enc parameters...
Recovered p: 
Recovered q: 
Step 2: Computing K^e modulo p and q...
Step 3: Isolating flag^e by multiplying with the inverse of K^e...
Step 4: Performing quaternion decryption via exponent inversion...
Recovered flag integer: 10604511300553789493465008259409401346333140435265134125395239474844319322789144251971024253
b'SK-CERT{RSA_w17h_u54g3_0f_qu473rn10n5}'
./solve.py  19.58s user 0.39s system 99% cpu 19.979 total
```

[Another write-up](https://hackmd.io/@deltaclock/SJATtnB16#RSA-40) says:

> The ratio between the i, j, and k components of the quaternion does not change under exponentiation. Therefore, we get
> a linear system that we can solve.

Implemented that in [`solve_linear_system.sage`](Solution/solve_linear_system.sage) and that works faster and
likely better:

```
% time sage solve_linear_system.sage 
b'SK-CERT{RSA_w17h_u54g3_0f_qu473rn10n5}'
sage solve_linear_system.sage  1.64s user 0.39s system 80% cpu 2.518 total
```

In the end, even ChatGPT managed to get it right, but it needed to know that:

> The ratio between the i, j, and k components of the quaternion does not change under exponentiation. Therefore, we get
> a linear system that we can solve.

And then I had to fix a few Sagemath errors. Its solution is in [`gpt.sage`](Solution/gpt.sage).

It also really helped to ask ChatGPT to add debugs around the code and provide it with that output, it really made a big
difference in its ability to spot what it did wrong. This was `o4-mini-high` and `o3`.

Other write-ups:

- https://7rocky.github.io/en/ctf/other/seccon-ctf/rsa-4.0/
- https://mcfx.us/posts/2023-09-18-seccon-2023-writeup/

```
SK-CERT{RSA_w17h_u54g3_0f_qu473rn10n5}
```

</details>
